coredata.dc:title: Silver-graphene quantum dots based electrochemical sensor for trinitrotoluene and p-nitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Jadoon, Tabish
Mahmood, Tariq
Ayub, Khurshid
coredata.dc:description: Silver cluster decorated graphene quantum dots act as efficient electrochemical sensors for the detection of nitroaromatic compounds due to their unique electronic properties. Herein, we report the first detailed theoretical study about the sensing ability of silver-graphene composites for the electrochemical detection of trinitrotoluene (TNT) & p-nitrophenol (p-NP). The complexes of TNT and p-NP with silver-graphene composite are quite stable as evident from their higher interaction energies (−15.64 to −70.07 kcal mol−1). The NBO charge analysis, DOS analysis, frontier molecular orbital analysis, UV–visible analysis, non-covalent interactions (NCI) & charge decomposition analysis (CDA) supported the interaction mechanism in TNT@Ag6-coronene & p-NP@Ag6-coronene complexes. NBO charge analysis results reveal that the charge is transferred from substrates to analytes except in p-NP@coronene and p-NP@coronene-Ag6 complexes. The generation of new energy states in DOS spectra reflect the chemical sensitivity of the system. Frontier molecular orbital analysis results validate a substantial decrease in the H-Lgap of silver-coronene composite on interaction with analytes. The shifting (red & blue) of λmax during interactions of analytes with silver-coronene is affirmed from excited state analysis. The NCI plots show that some steric repulsion is also there along with strong non-covalent electrostatic (M…O) & unusual van der Waals (M…H) interactions. The evaluated results of CDA validate the charge transfer interactions between various fragment pairs of complexes. The precedent results distinctly show the better sensing performance of silver-graphene composites towards TNT & p-NP.

head.ce:title: Silver-graphene quantum dots based electrochemical sensor for trinitrotoluene and p-nitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, COMSATS University Abbottabad Campus, 22060, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, COMSATS University Abbottabad Campus, 22060, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silver cluster decorated graphene quantum dots act as efficient electrochemical sensors for the detection of nitroaromatic compounds due to their unique electronic properties. Herein, we report the first detailed theoretical study about the sensing ability of silver-graphene composites for the electrochemical detection of trinitrotoluene (TNT) & p-nitrophenol (p-NP). The complexes of TNT and p-NP with silver-graphene composite are quite stable as evident from their higher interaction energies (−15.64 to −70.07 kcal mol−1). The NBO charge analysis, DOS analysis, frontier molecular orbital analysis, UV–visible analysis, non-covalent interactions (NCI) & charge decomposition analysis (CDA) supported the interaction mechanism in TNT@Ag6-coronene & p-NP@Ag6-coronene complexes. NBO charge analysis results reveal that the charge is transferred from substrates to analytes except in p-NP@coronene and p-NP@coronene-Ag6 complexes. The generation of new energy states in DOS spectra reflect the chemical sensitivity of the system. Frontier molecular orbital analysis results validate a substantial decrease in the H-Lgap of silver-coronene composite on interaction with analytes. The shifting (red & blue) of λmax during interactions of analytes with silver-coronene is affirmed from excited state analysis. The NCI plots show that some steric repulsion is also there along with strong non-covalent electrostatic (M…O) & unusual van der Waals (M…H) interactions. The evaluated results of CDA validate the charge transfer interactions between various fragment pairs of complexes. The precedent results distinctly show the better sensing performance of silver-graphene composites towards TNT & p-NP.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last fifteen decades, millions of tons of nitroaromatic materials have been released into the environment globally [1,2]. These nitroaromatic compounds have got remarkable attention due to their vast applications although some environmental hazards are also associated with them [3–9]. The contamination of soil and water due to the accidental release of these toxic and most widely used energetic explosive materials has become a matter of great concern [9–11]. Amongst these nitroaromatic compounds, 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) and 4-nitrophenol (p-NP or 4-NP) have been of significant concern due to their environmental hazards (toxic) and global threats (explosive) for life on this planet [3,11–16]. TNT and p-NP belong to the class of nitroaromatic compounds which have widespread applications in the field of explosives, pharmaceuticals, pigments, dyes, pesticides, wood preservatives, and rubber chemicals, homeland security, and humanitarian issues [17–22]. Despite its wide uses, TNT is a manmade nitroaromatic compound that stays for a long time in nature. TNT threatens human health as it is evident from recent terrorist's activities worldwide. Likewise, TNT, anthropogenic activities are the main contributors for the release of p-NP in the environment. This toxic compound may enter the atmosphere either by automobile exhaust or due to photochemical reaction between nitrogen monoxide and highly polluted air. The peoples have a great concern about the proper monitoring and control of TNT and p-NP, which are the main environmental culprits and global villains due to their toxic as well as explosive nature. For these reasons, there is a recent need to develop electrochemical sensors for the selective and accurate detection of TNT and p-NP.

Graphene quantum dots with excellent chemical and physical properties are quite promising electrochemical sensing materials for the detection of various analytes such as metal ions, gases and biological molecules [23–28]. The adsorption of analytes over zero band gap graphene causes little changes in its electronic structure at room temperature [29]. The reactivity of graphene is enhanced by modification of its surface properties through doping [30–33]. Substitutional and surface doping with transition metals especially coinage metal is an excellent method to open the band gap of graphene. Surface doping with transition metal is an efficient way for electron transfer between metal and graphene to improve its electrochemical properties [34–40]. Silver nanoparticles have a broad range of applications in sensing and catalytic devices due to their excellent electronic properties [41–45]. It has been recognized that silver metal-doped graphene composites contribute significantly to the detection of various analytes and find their broad applications in electrochemical sensing [41,42,46–53].

From the literature, it is quite clear that silver nanomaterial based graphene hybrid materials have been experimentally studied and find widespread applications for the electrochemical sensing of various gaseous analytes & biomolecules such as DNA, H2O2, CO2, NO, CO, NO2, TNT, p-NP, glucose, and ammonia etc.[42–56,58–61]. Theoretical investigations about the electrochemical sensing of TNT & p-NP over transition metal cluster doped graphene composites are very limited and particularly, for silver clusters decorated graphene hybrid is not known to date [62–69]. In our previous work, we performed a comprehensive theoretical study about the electronic properties of 2–10 atoms silver clusters decorated graphene composites [70]. In the present work, a first detailed theoretical study about electronic and sensing properties of silver cluster (Ag6) decorated graphene quantum dots is carried out which can find its ways towards the rational development of smart hybrid material for the electrochemical detection of TNT & p-NP.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations are performed with Gaussian 09, and visualization of results is carried out by GaussView 5.0 program package [71]. Spin polarized DFT study is used for the optimization of geometries. The geometry optimization is performed at most stable (singlet) spin multiplicity and vibrational frequencies are calculated to confirm that these optimized structures are true minima (with no imaginary frequency). The interaction energies of TNT & p-NP over the coplanar silver cluster (Ag6) doped coronene composite are analyzed first with M06-2X/LANL2DZ level. M06-2X is the best functional for accurate determination of interaction energies [72]. Several possible orientations for each analyte are considered to find the lowest energy structure. Interaction energies (E
int ), at M062X and M062X-D3 of analytes (TNT & p-NP) with coronene, pure silver cluster, and silver-coronene composite are calculated with Eqs. (1)–(3).

Natural bond orbital (NBO) analysis is performed at the same level of theory (M06-2X/LANL2DZ) to get a clear insight into the amount of charge transferred during interactions. The excited state calculations (UV–visible) are carried out with TD-DFT theory which describes all possible single excitations from occupied to virtual orbitals.

The DFT method has proven to be one of the most accurate methods for the computation of the electronic structure of solids [73–79]. The density of states (DOS) was analyzed by Gauss-Sum program. The DOS spectra with HOMO-LUMO visualization for the complexes are given in supporting information (Fig. S8b). To get knowledge about the probable electronic density distribution in the number of energy states available to the system, TDOS and PDOS are carried out with a Multiwfn 3.6 software package. Non-covalent interactions index (NCI) & charge decomposition analysis (CDA) is performed by Multiwfn to get knowledge about the nature of interactions.

Results of frequency calculations reveal that all the optimized structures are true minima with no imaginary frequencies. The most stable structures (for each orientation) of all optimized structures are used for properties calculations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction energies and geometric analysis for the complexes of TNT and p-NP with coronene
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of TNT & p-NP is carried out over the surface of isolated coronene (model graphene), and the results for all possible orientations are listed in Table (S1-S6) of the supporting information. The interaction energies along with the minimum bond distances between interacting atoms for most stable orientations are given in Table 1 . The minimum bond distance between the atom of the analyte and carbon atom of the coronene surface is reported to describe the strength of bonding in each case. The interaction energies of TNT & p-NP on coronene surface are −24.13 & −16.67 kcal mol−1, respectively. The minimum distances between analytes and coronene surface (C…H) for TNT & p-NP are 2.88 & 2.96 Å, respectively. Both TNT & p-NP have an aromatic ring, and anchoring of these analytes with carbon surface results from dispersive interactions due to deactivating groups on aromatic rings. The values of interaction energy and bond distance clarify that the adsorption of TNT is more favorable than p-NP on coronene surface. The greater stability of the TNT-coronene complex is due to the presence of greater number of deactivating nitro groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction energies and geometric analysis for the complexes of TNT and p-NP with silver cluster
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption behavior of TNT & p-NP with the silver cluster is mainly because of the M…O bond between interacting species. The interaction energies for the adsorption of TNT & p-NP are −49.16 and −34.23 kcal mol−1, respectively. The strength of the interaction is greater in the case of TNT adsorption over Ag6 than p-NP. The minimum Ag…O distances are 2.26 & 2.28 Å for TNT & p-NP, respectively (Fig. 1 ). The interaction energies of both TNT & p-NP are higher with silver cluster than with coronene ring mainly because of more electropositive character of silver metal that facilitates the strong bonding between analytes and metal atom. The adsorption energies of both TNT & p-NP on silver cluster are almost twice to that on coronene surface mainly because of strong electrostatic M…O interactions. The average Ag-Ag distance in a silver cluster is 2.90 Å which increases to 3.03 and 3.05 Å due to adsorption of TNT and p-NP, respectively over the pure silver cluster. Furthermore, The geometry of Ag6 changes from 2D to 3D on interaction with analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction energies and geometric analysis for the complexes of TNT and p-NP with silver-coronene composites
ce:section.ce:para: The results of geometric analysis and interaction energies for silver-coronene composite suggest that silver clusters bind with carbon surface through dispersion and charge transfer interactions, with calculated interaction energy of 23.26 kcal mol−1. To get a clear insight into the adsorption behavior of TNT & p-NP, two types of configurations 1 & 2 of silver-coronene composites are studied. The analyte directly interacts with the silver cluster side of silver-coronene composite (analyte@Ag6-coronene) in configuration 1 whereas the analyte is directly present over the coronene surface of silver-coronene composite (analyte@coronene-Ag6) in configuration 2.

The adsorption of TNT & p-NP over silver-coronene composite (configuration 1) results in interaction energies of −70.07 & −39.13 kcal mol−1, respectively. The minimum distances between analytes and silver atom of silver-coronene composite (Ag…O) for TNT and p-NP are 2.26 & 2.27 Å, respectively (Fig. 2 ). The interaction mechanism of TNT & p-NP with silver-coronene composite (configuration 2) results in the interaction energy of −22.66 & −15.64 kcal mol−1. The interaction energies of TNT are higher than those of p-NP with the carbon surface of the silver-coronene composite. The C…H distances of 2.85 & 2.98 Å are observed for TNT & p-NP, respectively. Geometric analysis reveals that the deformation of NO2 group is due to interaction of oxygen atoms of analytes with Ag atoms in analyte@Ag6 and analyte@Ag6-coronene complexes (configuration 1). In those cases where TNT and p-NP are interacting on graphene side (configuration 2), no such deformation is observed which is clearly indicating that silver atoms are causing this deformation.

The results of interaction energies reveal that the binding of TNT is more favorable than p-NP with pure silver cluster, coronene ring and silver-coronene composites due to more electron withdrawing (NO2) groups that facilitate interactions through charge transfer mechanism. The oxygen atom of analyte preferably interacts with Ag-atom whereas H-atom of analyte interacts with carbon atom of the substrate. Moreover, Ag…O bond (which results from strong electrostatic interactions) forms more suitable interactions than weak van der Waals dispersive interactions (C…H) between analytes and substrate.

The interaction energies calculated at M06-2X are corrected for basis set superposition error (BSSE) by Counterpoise method [80]. The resultant Counterpoise corrected interaction energies of the complexes, although follow the same trend, but are lower than those at M06-2X without BSSE correction. The highest BSSE correction is observed in TNT@Ag6-coronene (14.76 kcal mol−1), whereas the lowest is seen in the p-NP@coronene complex (4.33 kcal mol−1).

To account for dispersion correction, Grimme's dispersion correction approach is also adopted [81]. The interaction energies for the complexes of TNT & p-NP are calculated by the M062X-D3 method. Although, the interaction energies calculated with dispersion correction are higher, the trend of interaction energies of these complexes is the same as we observed with M06-2X, and BSSE corrected M06-2X. The highest dispersion correction is observed for TNT@coronene-Ag6 (3.35 kcal mol−1) whereas least for p-NP@Ag6 complex (0.41 kcal mol−1).

The results of interaction energies suggest that both analytes (TNT & p-NP) interact more firmly with silver-coronene composites than isolated silver clusters or coronene, due to the synergistic effect of both components in the composite. The order of stability in terms of interaction energies is TNT@Ag6-coronene > TNT@Ag6 > p-NP@Ag6-coronene > p-NP @Ag6 > TNT@coronene > TNT@coronene-Ag6 > p-NP@coronene > p-NP@coronene-Ag6.

To understand the mechanism of interactions between two species, charge analysis is very much important. Electronic and structural properties of interacting species change due to charge transfer between them. Natural bond orbital (NBO) charge analysis is carried out for TNT & p-NP adsorption over the isolated silver cluster, coronene, and silver-coronene composites, and results of charge analysis are summarized in Table 1.

The results of NBO charge analysis indicate that 1.34e − charge is transferred from co-planer silver cluster to p-NP in p-NP@Ag6 complex whereas 1.43e − amount of charge is transferred from TNT to silver cluster in TNT@Ag6 complex. The complexes of analytes with coronene substrates are evaluated via NBO charge analysis. The amount of charge transfer from p-NP to coronene is 0.006e − in the p-NP@coronene complex. In TNT@coronene complex, charge of 0.007e − is transferred from coronene to TNT. The charge transfer between analytes and silver cluster is dominating compared to the charge transfer between analytes and coronene surface.

The NBO charge transfer between p-NP & TNT with silver-coronene composite (configuration 1 & 2), shows that silver-coronene composite acts as a better sensing platform than isolated silver cluster and coronene. The amount of 2.00e − charge is transferred from silver-coronene composite to p-NP in the p-NP@Ag6-coronene complex (configuration 1). The charge transfer in TNT@Ag6-coronene complex (configuration 1) is 2.67e −. In complexes of both analytes with silver-coronene composite (configuration 1), the charge transfer towards analytes from the composite is noticed. The prominent charge transfer between analytes and configuration 1, is because of strong electrostatic interactions between them. On contrary to the configuration 1, less amount of charge transfer is observed between analytes and configuration 2 of silver-coronene composites. The less amount of charge transfer between analytes and composite in configuration 2, is due to dispersion interactions between them. The equal amount of charge (0.006e −) transferred is observed in both p-NP@coronene-Ag6 and p-NP@coronene complexes. Furthermore, the direction of charge transfer in both of these complexes is from analytes towards substrate. In TNT@coronene-Ag6 complex, charge transfer of 0.02e − is observed from silver-coronene composite towards analyte.

The order of charge transfer between silver-coronene composites and analytes follows the following order analyte@Ag6-coronene > analyte@Ag6 > analyte@coronene-Ag6 > analyte@coronene. The NBO charge transfer in the complexes of TNT is greater than complexes of p-NP. The greater amount of charge transfer is due to the high electrophilic nature of TNT. The greater number of electron withdrawing groups in TNT enable the charge transfer process more favorable than p-NP. The results of NBO charge analysis reveal that the interactions via charge transfer between analytes and silver-coronene composites are much more significant than over pure silver cluster and coronene. Transfer of electronic cloud between analytes and silver-coronene composites offers a good sensing platform for the detection of these analytes.

The FMO theory has been documented as the outstanding theory for the description of chemical reactivity of species. The interactions of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) with lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) is related to the frontier molecular orbital analysis and change in energy of HOMO and LUMO is a valuable indicator about sensing mechanism. The reactivity and stability of a species are characterized by a HOMO-LUMO gap. The species with smaller H-Lgap show high reactivity, high conductivity and are generally less stable. Frontier molecular orbital analysis is carried out in order to understand the reactivity of silver-coronene composite for the sensing of p-NP & TNT. The results of the FMO analysis are summarized in Table 2 . Lower the value of LUMO is indicative of its more susceptibility to accept electrons whereas higher energy of HOMO is indicative of its more susceptibility to loose electrons. The sensing mechanism of both analytes (p-NP & TNT) is investigated over the isolated coronene, pure silver cluster (Ag6) and silver-coronene composite.

The HOMO and LUMO energies for isolated coronene are −6.79 and −0.9 eV, respectively, with H-L gap of 5.89 eV. The value of the H-L gap decreased to 5.19 and 4.24 eV for p-NP@coronene & TNT@coronene complexes, respectively due to the overlap between orbitals of analytes and coronene. The energies of HOMO and LUMO decrease by the adsorption of analytes over coronene. The H-L gaps are decreased by 0.7 and 1.65 eV for p-NP@coronene and TNT@coronene, respectively when compared with isolated coronene. The decrease in the H-Lgap is more in case of TNT@coronene than p-NP@coronene.

The pure Ag6 has H-L gap of 4.02 eV which decreased to 3.51 and 3.73 eV for p-NP@Ag6 and TNT@Ag6 complexes, respectively. The decrease in H-L gap by the adsorption of analytes is due to the possible overlap between p-orbitals of analytes with d-orbital of silver metal. The energies of HOMO and LUMO decreases in case of TNT adsorption whereas, for p-NP, the energy of HOMO increases but the energy of LUMO decreases. The decrease in the value of the H-L gap is more in the case of p-NP@Ag6 than TNT@Ag6. H-L gaps of p-NP@Ag6 and TNT@Ag6 are decreased by 0.51 and 0.29 eV, respectively (compared to H-Lgap of pure silver cluster). The prominent decrease in the H-Lgap in the case of p-NP adsorption over silver cluster results from the strong overlapping between orbitals of Ag atom of the cluster with O atom of p-NP.

The silver-coronene composites is a promising adsorption material for the electrochemical sensing of p-NP and TNT. It is important here to mention that silver-coronene composite has an H-Lgap of 3.83 eV, which is lower than either pure silver cluster or coronene. The adsorption of p-NP and TNT over silver-coronene composite (configuration 1) results in H-Lgaps of 3.62 and 3.41 eV, respectively. The energies of both HOMO and LUMO decrease in the case of p-NP@Ag6-coronene and TNT@Ag6-coronene. The reduction in H-Lgaps for p-NP@Ag6-coronene and TNT@Ag6-coronene complexes as compared to silver-coronene are 0.21 and 0.42 eV, respectively. The decrease in the H-Lgap is more pronounced for TNT@Ag6-coronene than p-NP@Ag6-coronene which is due to the strong overlap between orbitals of oxygen and silver in the composite.

The observed H-Lgaps on adsorption of p-NP and TNT over silver-coronene composite (configuration 2) are 3.32 and 2.33 eV, respectively. The adsorption of p-NP and TNT results in decrease in energies of HOMOs and LUMOs. The H-Lgap for p-NP@coronene-Ag6 and TNT@coronene-Ag6 are decreased (in comparison with silver-coronene composite) by 0.51 and 1.5 eV, respectively. The decrease in the H-L gap is more in the case of TNT@coronene-Ag6 than p-NP@coronene-Ag6. The results of frontier molecular orbitals reveal that the sensing ability of configuration 2, is more dominating for both p-NP & TNT than configuration 1 of the silver-coronene composite. As chemical sensors work is based on the change of electrical conductivity or resistivity therefore, it is expected that the conductivity of silver-coronene composites is increased due to a decrease in the H-Lgap.

The density of state analysis is carried out to get insight into the interactions and charge transfer between analytes and silver-graphene composites. DOS fundamentally gives information about the number of various states at an energy level that electron is permitted to occupy. The number of available energy states for electron and changes that occur in the region of HOMO and LUMO provide useful information about the sensing abilities of the conducting materials. TDOS and PDOS spectra of TNT@Ag6-coronene and p-NP@Ag6-coronene are given in Fig. 3 . TDOS and PDOS spectra of other complexes are given in the supporting information (Fig. S8a). DOS spectra for silver clusters indicate that the HOMO and LUMO are present −5.76 & −1.74 eV with H-Lgap of 4.02 eV. On complexation of the silver cluster with TNT, the HOMO and LUMO peaks are shifted to −6.48 & −2.75 eV, respectively. The H-Lgap decreases due to a decrease in the energy of HOMO and LUMO during complexation. In the complex of p-NP with silver cluster, the HOMO and LUMO are shifted to −5.74 & −2.23 eV, respectively. The decrease in the H-Lgap in the p-NP@Ag6 complex is mainly due to the shifting of HOMO towards higher energy and LUMO towards lower energy compared to pure Ag6. The origination of new states and changes in peaks intensities in the vicinity of HOMO and LUMO reflect the interactions between analytes and silver cluster. PDOS spectra for the complexes of TNT@Ag6 and p-NP@Ag6 indicate that both analytes and silver cluster show major contribution in HOMO state.

The DOS spectra for coronene (see supporting information) reveal that the HOMO and LUMO peaks are located at −6.79 & −0.90 eV, respectively. The complexation of TNT with coronene results in the shifting of HOMO and LUMO peaks in the region of −7.14 & −2.89 eV, respectively. The H-Lgap is significantly reduced in TNT@coronene complex to 4.24 eV (compared to H-Lgap of coronene (5.89 eV). The reduction in the H-Lgap is due to the orbital interactions which causes the generation of new peaks in HOMO and LUMO at different energy levels. In p-NP@coronene complex, the HOMO and LUMO appear at −6.99 & −1.79 eV, respectively. The energies of HOMO and LUMO decreases which results in the reduction of H-Lgap to 5.19 eV in the complex. PDOS spectrum of TNT@coronene shows that coronene has a major contribution in HOMO state with some minor contribution from TNT. PDOS spectrum of p-NP@Ag6 indicate that both (analytes and silver cluster) show major contribution in HOMO state.

DOS spectra with HOMO-LUMO visualization of p-NP & TNT over silver-coronene composites (configuration 1 & 2) are given in Fig. 3. The HOMO and LUMO orbitals of silver-coronene are originally located at −5.17 & −1.34 eV, respectively with an H-Lgap of 3.83 eV. On complexation of TNT with silver-coronene composite (configuration 1), the HOMO and LUMO are shifted to −5.34 & −3.01 eV, respectively. The decrease in the H-Lgap to 2.33 eV is mainly due to the decrease in energy of LUMO on interactions. The interactions of p-NP with (in configuration 1) silver-coronene composite shifts the HOMO and LUMO peaks to −5.29 & −1.66 eV, respectively. The observed H-Lgap for the p-NP@coronene-Ag6 complex is 3.62 eV, which is greater than that of p-NP@coronene-Ag6 complex. For the complex of TNT (configuration 2) with silver-coronene composite, the HOMO and LUMO peaks appear at −5.34 & −3.01 eV, respectively. There is a significant decrease in the energy of LUMO during complexation which results in the reduction of H-Lgap to 2.33 eV. The HOMO and LUMO peaks for the complex of p-NP with configuration 2, appear at −5.27 & −1.95 eV, respectively. The variations in peaks at various energies of HOMO and LUMO affirm the interactions of p-NP with the silver-coronene composite.

It is clear from the DOS spectra that interactions of TNT & p-NP with silver- coronene composites result in remarkable decrease in the H-Lgap. The interactions between TNT & p-NP with silver-coronene are effective enough to produce significant variations in the electronic properties of the silver-coronene composites. The generation of new orbitals and variations in the intensity of peaks advocates the promising interactions of p-NP and TNT with silver-coronene composite. It can be seen from Fig. 3 that there are a number of virtual states appearing compared to occupied states, which clearly indicate high sensing ability of silver-coronene composites towards p-NP and TNT. The spectral visualization of PDOS shows that silver cluster and analytes have major contribution towards HOMO energy in TNT@Ag6-coronene and p-NP@Ag6-coronene complexes. In TNT@coronene-Ag6 and p-NP@coronene-Ag6 complexes, only the silver cluster component. mainly contributes to the energy of HOMO whereas the other components show minor contribution.

Literature reveals that UV–vis spectroscopic studies are important to get insight into electronic properties in sensing materials. UV–vis analysis is carried out with TD-DFT and results are summarized in Table 2. The UV–vis spectral visualization for the adsorption of p-NP &TNT over configuration 1 of silver-coronene composites, pure silver cluster, and isolated coronene are given in Figs. 4–6
. The energy required for electronic transition is excitation energy while the oscillator strength (f) is the intensity of these transitions. The λmax, the absorption maxima which is associated with crucial or dominant transitions from occupied to virtual orbitals are mentioned here in Table 2. The λmax of 262 nm is related to the main transition from HOMO−1 → LUMO in coronene with the excitation energy of 4.71 eV. The adsorption of p-NP over coronene results in slight increase in λmax value to 264 nm with excitation energy of 4.69 eV. The main transition in the case of p-NP@coronene is from HOMO−7 → LUMO. This slight shifting of λmax indicates the weak interaction of p-NP with coronene. The λmax is more red-shifted in case of TNT adsorption over coronene which suggests that TNT interacts with coronene more favorably than p-NP. The observed λmax for TNT@ coronene is 272 nm, with excitation energy of 4.54 eV. This excitation energy is associated with dominant transitions from HOMO−6 → LUMO.

The observed λmax of pure silver cluster is 394 nm which is in quite good agreement with an experimentally reported value of 397 nm [85]. The excitation energy for the crucial transition (from HOMO → LUMO+2) in pure silver cluster is 3.14 eV. On contrary to adsorption of analytes over coronene, the adsorption of these analytes over pure silver cluster results in more bathochromic or red shifting of λmax. The adsorption of p-NP over silver cluster results in shifting of λmax towards the longer wavelength at 419 nm. The excitation energy decreases to 2.95 eV in the case of p-NP@Ag6 than pure Ag6, which is a consequence of the dominant transition from HOMO−1 → LUMO+1. The λmax for TNT@Ag6 is 409 nm, which is slightly less than p-NP@Ag6, with excitation energy of 3.02 eV. The more excitation energy for the TNT‑silver complex indicates its less favorable interactions with pure silver clusters than p-NP. The prominent transition in the case of TNT adsorption over the silver cluster is also from HOMO−1 → LUMO+1.

The composites of silver-coronene are investigated to check their electronic properties in comparison with isolated coronene and pure silver cluster. The Ag6-coronene composite shows λmax of 435 nm with excitation energy value 2.84 eV which is related to the dominant transition from HOMO−1 → LUMO+4. The λmax value for silver-coronene composite is in reasonable agreement with the experimentally reported value of 425 nm of silver nanoparticles decorated graphene hybrid material [86]. The band at 435 nm is related to the distinctive adsorption of the silver cluster over the coronene surface which is not observed in the case of isolated coronene. The UV–Vis spectroscopic studies reveal that Ag6-coronene composites are better sensing platforms for the detection of p-NP and TNT than isolated coronene and pure silver cluster. Moreover, the results of UV–Vis spectroscopic analysis for configuration 1, of silver-coronene composites show remarkable interactions with analytes than configuration 2.

The λmax values of 432 & 430 nm are calculated for p-NP@coronene-Ag6 & TNT@coronene-Ag6, respectively. The λmax is slightly blue shifted on interactions of p-NP and TNT in configuration 2 of the silver-coronene composite. The excitation energies in the case of p-NP@coronene-Ag6 and TNT@coronene-Ag6 are 2.86 & 2.87 eV, respectively. The prominent transition is from HOMO−1 → LUMO+1 for p-NP@coronene-Ag6, whereas HOMO−1 → LUMO+4 is the crucial excitation for TNT@coronene-Ag6.The sensing abilities of silver-coronene composites in configuration 1, are much more fascinating for the detection of p-NP & TNT. The adsorption of both analytes results in significant shifting of λmax to longer wavelength. The λmax for the adsorption of p-NP & TNT over configuration 1, are 467 & 468 nm, respectively. The excitation energy of 2.65 eV is associated with the crucial transition from HOMO−1 → LUMO+2 in p-NP@Ag6-coronene. In the case of TNT@Ag6-coronene, a dominant transition is from HOMO−1 → LUMO+2 with excitation energy of 2.64 eV. It is worth mentioning here that the larger bathochromic shift of λmax is observed for the interactions of both p-NP & TNT with configuration 1, of a silver-coronene composite which is attributed to its higher sensitivity. From UV–Vis spectra, a close analysis reveals that interaction of silver cluster with nitro compounds is the signaling part because interaction of analytes with coronene only does not bring any significant change in the absorption maxima except that the intensity is slightly changed. For interaction of analytes on Ag6 or Ag6-coronene, not only the absorption coefficient is changed but also the absorption maxima are displaced.

Non-Covalent Interactions index is a tool based on the density and its derivatives, which is widely used now a days to get an understanding of non-covalent interactions such as van der Waals interactions, hydrogen bonding, and steric clashes. NCI is a dynamic technique for the identification and classification of the noncovalent interactions in complexes based on the RDG (reduce density gradient). The RDG is calculated by taking the first derivative of electron density ρ(r).
RDG =

π 2
1 / 3
∣ ∇ ρ
r
∣
ρ
r
4 / 3

Where ρ and ∇ρ represent the electronic density and electronic density gradient, respectively. The RDG is a dimensionless function which is used to define the deviation from homogenous electron distribution. The reduced gradient has a very large positive value far from the molecule, as electron density dies off there exponentially to zero. The RDG has very small values, close to zero, in the region of covalent bonding and non-covalent interactions [82–84]. The explanation of the noncovalent interactions via the NCI index, the colored iso-surfaces are obtained on RDG iso-surfaces by mapping sign (λ2) ρ(r). Literature reveals that the RDG iso-surface is based on a color scheme code: red-yellow-green-blue. Red color represents the steric clashes, the blue color indicates non-covalent attractive forces while green and yellow colors show weak attractive and weak repulsive forces, respectively. To determine the type of interactions, three eigenvalues (λi) of Hessian, the second derivative of electron- density are calculated (λ1 ≤ λ2 ≤ λ3). At nuclei, as the density is at the local maximum, all the eigen values are negative. For covalent interactions, two of the eigen values of Hessian are negative while the third one is positive. On the other hand, in the region of steric clashes or strain, the second eigen value is positive, and this value is used to distinguish between bonded and non-bonded interactions. For bonded interactions, the eigen value λ2 < 0, and for non-bonded destabilizing repulsive interactions the eigen value λ2 > 0. The strength of non-covalent interactions is directly related to the density, as the higher value of density indicates the stronger interactions and vice versa. NCI plot with iso-surface visualization (configuration 1) for the adsorption of TNT and p-NP over the surface of the Ag6-coronene composite is given in Fig. 7 , while the rest of the structures are given in supporting information (Fig. N1–N6). The spikes in the NCI plot are associated with each possible interaction in a composite, and the shifting of spike either towards negative or positive λ2ρ values by the interactions of analytes is clearly observed from Fig. 7.

In the NCI plot, the blue, green and red spikes represent strong non-covalent interactions, weak non-covalent interactions, and steric repulsions, respectively. The RGD surfaces show a spatial arrangement of interactions and color scale gives the strength of interactions during complexation. RDG scatter map of TNT@Ag6 complex affirm that low density, low gradient peaks appear at λ2ρ = −0.03, 0.01–0.02 and = −0.01 a.u which are associated with strong non-covalent attachment such as electrostatic (M…O), the existence of steric clashes and insignificant M…H interactions, respectively (Fig. S12 of supporting information).. In p-NP@Ag6 complex, peaks appeared at λ2ρ value of −0.03, −0.02 & 0.01–0.02 a.u (Fig. S9 of supporting information). These peaks are similar to that of TNT@Ag6 complex and indicate the same types of interactions. In addition, blue-green disc-like NCI isosurface is observed between the silver cluster and analytes which also indicates weak van der Waals interactions in both complexes.

NCI results reveal that, in TNT@coronene & p-NP@coronene complexes, analyte interacts with coronene surface mostly through weak non-covalent (hydrophobic) interactions and small contributions from strong non-covalent (H-bond) interactions. In both complexes, peaks appeared at λ2ρ = −0.01 & λ2ρ = 0.01–0.02 a.u, corresponds to van der Waals interactions and steric repulsions, respectively.

The NCI analysis for TNT@Ag6-coronene complex (configuration 1) shows that three distinctive peaks appear at λ2ρ = −0.03, −0.01 & 0.02 a.u which are associated with strong electrostatic interactions, van der Waals interactions, and steric clashes, respectively. In p-NP@Ag6-coronene complex (configuration 1), three distinctive spikes appeared at λ2ρ = −0.04 to −0.02, λ2ρ = 0.01 and −0.01 a.u which are associated with M…O electrostatic interactions, steric clashes, and insignificant M…H van der Waals interactions. In both TNT@coronene-Ag6 and p-NP@coronene-Ag6 complexes, distinctive peaks appeared at λ2ρ = −0.03, −0.01 and 0.02 a.u associated with non-covalent electrostatic (M…O) interactions, uncommon M…H-O attractive forces, and steric repulsions, respectively.

Charge decomposition analysis (CDA) is an unraveling technique that is used to analyze the interactions between donor and acceptor fragments in the complex. CDA is a useful method for the assessment of relative strength of charge donation and back-donation in the complex. The results of CDA are summarized in Table 3 and Fig. 8 . Here we focus on the relative amount of forward donation and repulsion in the composite. The relative strength of charge donation of −0.47 and −0.38 e − are observed in TNT@Ag6 & p-NP@Ag6, respectively. In these complexes, charge transfer via back donation from d-orbital of silver to p-orbital of analytes is dominating. The silver metal has d10 electronic configuration, and it does not favor charge transfer trough σ-donation. The repulsion of charges is an important indicator of charge density between donor and acceptor fragments. The values of repulsion observed for TNT@Ag6 & p-NP@Ag6 are −0.08 & −0.10 e −, respectively which indicate that charge density is moving away from the interaction region in these complexes.

In TNT@coronene & p-NP@coronene complexes, relative donation of charges is 0.02 & −0.02 e −, respectively. The amount of π-donation of charge from coronene to TNT is greater than π-back donation in TNT@coronene complex. On the other hand, the amount of π-back donation of charge from coronene to p-NP is greater than π-donation in the p-NP@coronene complex. The repulsion values of −0.25 & −0.20e −, are observed in TNT@coronene & p-NP@coronene complexes, respectively. These values indicate that charge density is moving away from the interaction region between fragments.

The results of CDA for the adsorption of TNT@Ag6-coronene complex (configuration 1) reveals that the relative donation of charges of 0.00, −0.18 & 0.01e − are observed between TNT-Ag6, Ag6-coronene & TNT-coronene fragments, respectively. The π-donation of charges from the d-orbital of silver is greater than back donation in the case of TNT-Ag6 fragment. A similar charge transfer trend is also observed for TNT-coronene fragment where π-donation of charges from p-orbital of the analyte to p-orbital of coronene is greater than the π-back donation. In the case of Ag6-coronene fragment, there is a greater π-back donation of charge from d-orbital of silver is observed than a donation. The repulsion of charges found between TNT-Ag6, Ag6-coronene & TNT-coronene fragments of silver-coronene composites is −0.00, 0.16 & 1.09 e −, respectively. The charge density is concentrated in Ag6-coronene & TNT-coronene fragments whereas it is moving off in the interaction region between TNT-coronene fragment.

In p-NP@Ag6-coronene complex, the relative donation of charges of −0.45, −0.06 & 0.00 e − are observed between p-NP-Ag6, Ag6-coronene & p-NP-coronene fragments, respectively. The greater back donation of charge from d-orbital of silver than donation is observed in p-NP-Ag6 & Ag6-coronene whereas greater π-donation is observed in p-NP-coronene fragments. The values of repulsion (of charges) for p-NP-Ag6, Ag6-coronene & p-NP-coronene fragments of complexes are −0.10, −0.12 & −0.00 e −, respectively which indicates that charge density is moving away from the interaction region in these fragments.

CDA results for TNT@coronene-Ag6 complex reveals that the relative amount of charge donation between TNT-Ag6, Ag6-coronene & TNT-coronene fragments pair are −0.01, 0.58 & 1.24 e −, respectively. In the TNT-Ag6 fragment, the amount of charge transfer through π-back donation from the d-orbital of silver is greater than donation. For other fragments pair Ag6-coronene & TNT-coronene, the amount of charge transfer through π-donation dominates over back donation. In Ag6-coronene fragment, the charge is donated from p-orbital of carbon to metal d-orbitals and in TNT-coronene fragment, the charge is transferred via a donation from p-orbital of coronene to p-orbital of TNT due to greater number of its electron withdrawing groups. The values of repulsion for TNT-Ag6 & Ag6-coronene are −0.00 & −0.31 e −, respectively. These values of repulsion indicate that the charge density is moving away from the interaction region. The value of repulsion of charges for TNT-coronene fragment is 1.22 e − which indicates that there is an accumulation of charge density in the interaction region.

In p-NP@coronene-Ag6 complex, the relative amount of charge donation of −0.00, −0.11& 0.00 e − are observed between p-NP-Ag6, Ag6-coronene & p-NP-coronene fragments. The charge transfer trough π-back donation is dominant in p-NP-Ag6, Ag6-coronene fragments because of d10. configuration of silver metal. In p-NP-coronene fragment pair, the amount of charge transfer via π-donation from p-orbital of the analyte to p-orbital of coronene is greater than a π-back donation. The value of repulsion for p-NP-Ag6, Ag6-coronene & p-NP-coronene fragments of p-NP@coronene-Ag6 complex are 0.00, −0.12& −0.02 e −, respectively. The value of repulsion of charges for a p-NP-Ag6 fragment indicates that charge density is concentrated in the binding region whereas for Ag6-coronene & p-NP-coronene fragments pair it is moving away from the binding region. CDA demonstrates that charge transfer via back donation dominates in analyte-Ag6 fragments pair of the complexes. Silver has d10 configuration and does not favors charge transfer through donation and charge is transferred from d-orbital of silver to analyte p-orbital. In analyte-coronene fragments pair of the complexes charge transfer via donation dominates over back donation.

Silver-graphene composites are the promising material widely used for the sensitive and accurate detection of nitroaromatic compounds. We demonstrate here silver-graphene composites for the electrochemical detection of TNT & p-NP due to their unique electronic properties. The TNT & p-NP adsorbed silver-coronene composites show higher stability & greater charge transfer as compared to pure silver cluster and coronene. Analytes bind with silver-coronene composites via charge-transfer interactions to form stable Ag…O anchoring bonds. The interaction of TNT & p-NP with silver-coronene composites are effective enough to reduce the H-Lgap in a range from 2.33 to 3.62 eV. The overlap between HOMO & LUMO significantly decreases the excitation energy from 4.69 to 2.64 eV in silver-coronene composites. The red shifting of λmax observed during interactions of analytes with coronene, pure silver cluster & silver-coronene (configuration 1), while blue shifting of λmax for silver-coronene (configuration 2) during excitation from occupied to virtual orbitals. The alteration in the density of charges at various energy intervals of TDOS spectrum & charge transfer interactions via donation and back donation between various fragments supports good sensitivity of silver-graphene composites towards TNT & p-NP. The current DFT study, to the best of our knowledge, has never been reported in the literature and this will be helpful for the exploration of smart silver cluster decorated graphene sensors for various practical applications.

Tabish Jadoon: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Tariq Mahmood: Visualization, Investigation, Software, Validation. Khurshid Ayub: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.
